params.text:
The difference between assertiveness and aggression

Being assertive is one of the most useful skills in conflict resolution — and it is very different from being aggressive. Assertiveness means expressing your needs, limits, and views clearly and respectfully, while still valuing the other person. Its underlying message is "I matter, and you matter too."

Aggression, by contrast, tries to dominate or overpower — through shouting, threats, or intimidation — and sends the message "I matter, and you don't." Passivity is the opposite problem: giving in and not stating your needs at all, which can leave issues unresolved.

  • Assertive — calm, clear, honest, respects both people's rights.
  • Aggressive — loud, hostile, blaming, ignores the other person's feelings.
  • Passive — avoids the issue, suppresses your own needs, may build resentment.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Assertive or aggressive? Tap each card to reveal the assertive alternative.

dialogs.text: "You never listen to a word I say!"
dialogs.answer: Assertive version: "I feel I'm not being heard, and it's important to me. Can we go through this together?"

dialogs.text: "Do it now, I haven't got time for this."
dialogs.answer: Assertive version: "I'm a little short on time, so let's agree what we can sort out right now and what can wait."
Assertive body language: steady calm tone, relaxed open posture, respectful eye contact, and a comfortable distance.
